ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for karaage; 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest defensible parent class. Karaage is a Japanese-style marinated deep-fried chicken preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible match. No karaage-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to karaage or Japanese fried chicken as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of karaage is approximately 4–5 pieces (150 g), commonly served as a side dish, appetizer, or bento component.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, fried (SR Legacy ID 05068) adapted for karaage preparation with coating and marinade
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken thigh (meat only) was used as the primary anchor. Values were adapted to account for the karaage preparation: potato starch and wheat flour coating (adds ~3–5 g carbohydrate per 100 g), soy sauce marinade (adds ~400–500 mg sodium per 100 g), and deep-frying in vegetable oil (increases fat to ~18–22 g per 100 g). The input record values (protein 18.67 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g, energy 300 kcal, sodium 500 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted profile and are retained. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a karaage-specific entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken (GI ~45) from University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No karaage-specific GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with battered/breaded fried chicken entries in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). The potato starch and wheat flour coating has a moderate GI, but the high protein and fat content of the dish substantially blunts the glycaemic response, supporting a low overall GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 45 × (10.0 × 150 / 100) / 100 = 45 × 15 / 100 = 6.75.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (500 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit karaage to small portions (≤75 g) and request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the marinade, as a standard 150 g serving delivers 750 mg sodium — approximately 33% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate from starch coating and high caloric density from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Karaage has a low estimated GI (~45) due to high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying, but the high caloric density (300 kcal/100 g) and deep-fried preparation can worsen insulin resistance if consumed in large portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 100 g and avoid pairing with high-GI carbohydrate sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20 g/100 g), saturated fat (4 g/100 g), cholesterol (66.67 mg/100 g), and sodium (500 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and soy sauce marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Karaage is high in total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium — all established cardiovascular risk factors — and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions (≤75 g) and prefer air-fried or baked preparations to reduce fat and caloric load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried karaage generates AGEs that directly exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should request baked or air-fried preparations and retain the anti-inflammatory ginger and garlic marinade components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Karaage (唐揚げ) is a popular Japanese deep-fried chicken dish in which bite-sized pieces of chicken thigh are marinated in a seasoned mixture of soy sauce, sake, mirin, ginger, and garlic, then coated in potato starch or a potato starch–wheat flour blend and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The result is a juicy, tender interior with a light, shatteringly crisp exterior and a savoury umami flavour profile with subtle sweetness from the mirin. Karaage is a staple of Japanese home cooking, izakaya menus, bento boxes, and convenience store offerings, and has become widely popular across Southeast Asia including Singapore. Per 100 g, karaage provides approximately 300 kcal, 18.7 g protein, 20 g fat (4 g saturated), 10 g carbohydrate, and 500 mg sodium. The high sodium content from the soy sauce marinade makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The high caloric and fat density from deep-frying is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, obesity, or cardiovascular risk. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption. A typical serving is 4–5 pieces (approximately 150 g), delivering approximately 450 kcal and 750 mg sodium.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in karaage?
What is the difference between karaage and regular fried chicken?

confidence_reason: No karaage-specific entry exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken thigh data with adjustments for karaage preparation (starch coating, soy sauce marinade, deep-frying). The input record values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with the USDA-adapted profile and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for karaage as a prepared dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; the USDA entry for fried chicken thigh (SR Legacy ID 05068) does not include a full micronutrient breakdown applicable to the karaage preparation with marinade and starch coating. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for fried chicken preparations; deep-frying destroys residual vitamin C from marinade ingredients. The input record ethnic_tags included 'Ramen' and 'Noodle' which are incorrect for karaage and have been removed. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore' which is incorrect; karaage originates from Japan and this has been corrected. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore but does not constitute an approved nutrient source for karaage; USDA FoodData Central has been used as the primary approved source instead.
